The CLI's `--verbose` flag on init/scan creates a verbose log that includes thinking content. The progress callback shows running tok/s: `[X/Y] ✓ adrId (+Z edges) tok/s`. This aligns with ADR-015's thinking block extraction and running average tok/s display.

The `check` command exists with full implementation. It reads the DAG, enumerates ADR→Module bindings, evaluates each using LLM, and displays status (aligned/drift/broken) with evidence. It supports `adr-graph check`, `adr-graph check ADR-001`, and file path targeting — matching ADR-017.

The check command has `--all` flag to include previously possibly-related bindings and `--changed` flag for git-aware filtering. The `checkAll` and `filterModuleIds` options are passed to `checkDrift()`, implementing check-time filtering as specified in ADR-019.

The check output displays three tiers: green "✅ [aligned]", yellow "⚠️  [drifting]", red "🔴 [broken]" for related bindings, cyan "?[possibly related]" for possibly_related, and gray "✖ [unrelated — removed]" for unrelated. It also shows "Unrelated: X" and "Possibly: Y" in summary — matching ADR-021.

The check command calls `checkDrift(dag, adrs, client, {...})` which groups bindings by module in the semantic layer. The CLI passes filter options and receives batch results including bindings, token metrics, and pruned/possibly-related edge IDs, implementing code-centric batch checking as per ADR-022.

The module implements treemap layout via computeDirLayout() using d3.treemap(). Directory paths are parsed into a hierarchy, external packages are placed in a dedicated strip at the bottom (height extH = 60), and the treemap area is proportional to node count.

The module implements click-to-inspect with directional edge coloring (green #7ee787 for outgoing, orange #ffa657 for incoming), edges hidden by default in treemap mode (link.attr("display", "none") when dirLayoutActive), uniform grid arrangement within cells, drag constrained to cell bounds in tick handler, and double-click resets position to original grid location.

The module implements edge tooltips on hover via linkHit mouse handlers showing "Source → Target" label, edge kind, certainty ("inferred (LLM)" for inferred edges), and reason text. Hit-area lines (linkHit) provide 12px-wide targeting, visibility syncs with syncHitVisibility(). The legend includes "inferred (LLM)" dashed line and filter checkboxes include both "affects" and "belongs_to".

The ModuleNode interface explicitly includes `language: "typescript" | "python" | "external" | "unknown"`, which directly implements the ADR-005 decision to add an "external" value to distinguish external package nodes from source modules.

The GraphEdge interface correctly implements ADR-011: the confidence field is optional (`confidence?: number`) and is only used when certainty is "inferred". The reason field exists in EdgeMetadata for evidence-based explanations. The types align with removing required confidence scores.

The Relevance type is defined exactly as `"related" | "possibly_related" | "unrelated"`, which directly implements the three-tier classification from ADR-021. The EdgeMetadata interface includes the optional relevance field.

The prompt.ts module implements the module filtering heuristic from ADR-009: it excludes external packages (line 25: `m.language !== "external"`), filters by path-keyword overlap with ADR title/body (lines 28-40), boosts modules matching frontmatter `affects` paths (lines 48-53), and caps at MAX_MODULES=80. The prompt includes ADR text, module list, and existing edges as specified.

The InferredEdge interface (lines 13-18) has no confidence field — only kind, from, to, and reason. The prompt instructions (line 101) require "The 'reason' must cite specific evidence: quote relevant ADR text" and (line 103) "Only include relationships you are confident about. When in doubt, omit." — matching ADR-011's requirement to remove confidence scoring and strengthen evidence-based reasoning.

The moduleToLine function (lines 57-65) includes imports in each module line: "imports: ..." with up to 10 import paths. The prompt rules explicitly include: tightened "implements" definition requiring direct code changes, import verification rule ("Use the imports list to verify relationships"), barrel file exclusion ("Do NOT mark barrel/re-export files (index.ts) as 'implements'"), test file exclusion, and specific evidence requirement in reason field.

The module src/core/semantic/index.ts is the entry point for the semantic layer that implements ADR-009's semantic edge inference. It exports createSemanticClient, analyzeSemantics, and checkDrift - the core functions for calling the Anthropic API and performing LLM-based semantic analysis as specified in ADR-009's decision to use Direct Anthropic API.

[UNRELATED] ADR-009 is about using @anthropic-ai/sdk for semantic edge inference (LLM integration). The code-summarizer.ts module is about ADR-aware code summarization (keyword extraction, block splitting, budget allocation). It does not import or use any LLM SDK, nor does it have any relationship to semantic edge inference. The binding should not exist.

The module directly implements all three stages of ADR-018's summarization pipeline: (1) extractKeywords extracts title words, backtick-wrapped terms, and double-quoted identifiers; (2) splitIntoBlocks splits code at import statements and top-level declarations; (3) summarizeForCheck performs IDF-weighted budget allocation with imports included first, highest-scoring blocks expanded, others collapsed. The function scoreBlocks (line 28) implements IDF-weighted keyword relevance scoring.

ADR-019 explicitly states "reusing `extractKeywords` from ADR-018's code-summarizer" for check-time relevance filtering. The module provides extractKeywords (used by summarizeForCheck for IDF-weighted scoring), which can be reused to compute keyword relevance scores at check time. The module's summarization logic already implements the keyword-based prioritization strategy described in ADR-019.

ADR-024 requires implementing buildCodeOverview (returns imports + block signatures, no bodies) and readCodeBlock (finds block by name, returns full body) with small file optimization (under 6000 chars). The module has both: buildCodeOverview checks SMALL_FILE_THRESHOLD (6000) and returns complete text if under threshold, otherwise returns block signatures; readCodeBlock matches block names against signatures and returns full body. The implementation matches the ADR requirements exactly.

The module uses @anthropic-ai/sdk (Anthropic import), implements graceful degradation via createSemanticClient() returning null when no credentials are found, and uses model defaults (ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"). This aligns with ADR-009's core decision to use direct Anthropic API with environment variable detection.

The module implements provider-switching (createSemanticClient checks LIGARE_ANTHROPIC_KEY → AWS_REGION → ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT+CLOUD_ML_REGION → LIGARE_COMPATIBLE_KEY+URL), supports forced provider via LIGARE_PROVIDER, and model override via LIGARE_MODEL. The four providers (anthropic, bedrock, vertex, compatible) share the same buildClient function using the three SDKs. Progress callback is appropriately implemented at CLI level, not in the client itself.

The AnalyzeResult interface includes inputTokens, outputTokens, and durationMs fields. The buildClient function reads response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens, and measures durationMs via Date.now() delta. This directly implements ADR-012's requirement for token metrics in AnalyzeResult.

The buildClient.analyze() method uses response.content.find((b: any) => b.type === "text") to correctly extract the text block, skipping thinking blocks. This fixes the bug described in ADR-013 where content[0] was read directly, which would have returned the thinking block instead of the actual response.

The AnalyzeResult interface includes truncated: boolean, set to true when response.stop_reason === "max_tokens". MAX_TOKENS is set to 4096 (increased from 1024 per ADR-014). Dotenv loading is correctly handled at CLI entry point, not in this client module. This aligns with ADR-014's truncation detection requirement.

The AnalyzeResult interface includes optional thinking?: string field. The buildClient.analyze() method extracts thinking via response.content.find((b: any) => b.type === "thinking"). Running average tok/s calculation is appropriately handled at CLI level (the client provides raw token data; the CLI computes the running average). This aligns with ADR-015's thinking block extraction requirement.

This binding is unrelated.

The module implements analyzeWithTools() method with the exact signature specified in ADR-023 (system, userMessage, tools, toolHandler). ToolDefinition interface matches the required schema. ToolUseResult includes toolCalls array with name and input fields. The multi-turn loop in buildClient.analyzeWithTools() handles tool_use blocks, sends tool_result responses, and continues until stop_reason is not "tool_use". This directly implements Phase 1 of ADR-023's tool-use based drift check.

The module uses SemanticClient from ./client which wraps the Anthropic API. The checkDrift function calls client.analyzeWithTools() to make API calls, making the module's behavior constrained by the direct API decision.

The checkDrift function is the core implementation of the adr-graph check command. It enumerates bindings, builds prompts with ADR text and source code, evaluates status (aligned/drifting/broken), and returns results — exactly as ADR-017 specifies.

The binding type is 'implements' but the actual code summarization logic (keyword extraction, IDF-weighted block selection) resides in code-summarizer.ts. The checker.ts only imports and delegates to readCodeBlock. Per the rules, 'Delegation is NOT implementation' — the module uses summarization but doesn't directly implement it.

The code implements check-time filtering: lines in checkDrift filter bindings based on opts.checkAll and check edge.metadata.relevance !== 'possibly_related', skipping low-relevance bindings by default.

The code handles three-tier relevance classification. Results are processed checking drift.relevance for 'unrelated', 'possibly_related', or 'related' values, with different handling and result counts for each tier.

The groupByModule function groups bindings by module (code-centric), and checkDrift iterates over these groups, sending one LLM call per module containing all governing ADRs chronologically — exactly matching ADR-022's code-centric batch check.

The code uses tool-use: imports SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL, builds tools array, uses toolHandler for tool call resolution, and parses results via parseToolCallResults — implementing ADR-023's structured output via submit_verdict tool.

The code implements on-demand reading: conditionally adds READ_CODE_TOOL when codeComplete is false, implements toolHandler that calls readCodeBlock(filePath, blockName), and the multi-turn flow returns full code on read_code calls.

[UNRELATED] ADR-009 is about semantic edge inference during scan (discovering ADR↔Module relationships). This module builds prompts for drift checking (evaluating existing bindings). The check-prompt module doesn't perform inference or call Anthropic API directly—it delegates to SemanticClient. These are distinct functions.

This module implements ADR-017's drift detection mechanism. It builds the prompts that send module source code + ADR text to the LLM for evaluation. The buildCheckUserMessage function creates the user message containing ADR bodies, module code, and binding list—the core mechanism of ADR-017's check command.

The module imports and uses buildCodeOverview from code-summarizer.ts (line 5 of imports). In buildCheckUserMessage, it calls buildCodeOverview(mod.filePath) to get the code section with collapsed blocks, exactly as ADR-018 specifies for ADR-aware summarization.

[POSSIBLY_RELATED] ADR-019 specifies keyword-based relevance filtering (computeRelevance function). This module doesn't implement keyword filtering—it uses ADR-021's three-tier classification via submit_verdict tool output. The system prompt references relevance tiers but the specific ADR-019 filtering mechanism is handled elsewhere.

The SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL explicitly includes relevance field with enum ["related", "possibly_related", "unrelated"] matching ADR-021's three tiers. The parseToolCallResults function validates against VALID_RELEVANCE set containing these three values. The system prompt defines each tier's meaning.

The buildCheckUserMessage function implements code-centric grouping—it takes a single module (mod) and arrays all its governing ADRs (adrBindings), exactly as ADR-022 specifies. The system prompt includes ADR evolution instruction: "Later ADRs may extend, modify, or supersede earlier ones...evaluate the earlier ADR as aligned."

The module literally exports SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL with the exact schema from ADR-023: name "submit_verdict", required fields ["adr_id", "status", "relevance", "reason"], status enum ["aligned", "drifting", "broken"], relevance enum ["related", "possibly_related", "unrelated"]. This is the core implementation of ADR-023.

The module exports READ_CODE_TOOL with exact schema from ADR-024: name "read_code", required ["block_name"], description matches ADR-024. The buildCheckUserMessage function calls buildCodeOverview to produce the initial skeleton with collapsed blocks, and the system prompt instructs LLM to use read_code when blocks are collapsed—exactly ADR-024's mechanism.

The analyzer.ts module orchestrates semantic analysis using SemanticClient which wraps the Anthropic API. It implements batch inference during scan (analyzes active ADRs), uses filterRelevantModules for module filtering heuristic, and preserves existing edges on re-scan by checking `dag.edges[eid]` before adding new inferred edges.

The analyzer implements progress reporting via the AnalysisProgress interface with onProgress callback. It fires "analyzing", "done", and "error" status updates with current/total count, ADR ID, and edgesAdded. The provider-switching logic is delegated to SemanticClient (imported from ./client.js).

The code creates inferred edges with certainty: "inferred" and metadata: { reason: edge.reason }, but does NOT set a confidence field. The parseAnalysisResponse result is used directly without any confidence threshold filtering - edges are added based on whether they already exist in the DAG.

While the CLI displays results as "ADR ↔ module" pairs, the internal grouping (module-centric vs ADR-centric) is handled by checkDrift. The function receives all ADRs and can group bindings by module for batch LLM calls per ADR-022's code-centric approach.

The generateHTML function directly implements the D3.js visualization - it creates the HTML template string with D3.js CDN import, constructs the force-directed graph using d3.forceSimulation, and outputs a complete self-contained HTML file. No delegation to other modules.

The generateHTML function directly implements the D3.js visualization - it creates the HTML template string with D3.js CDN import, constructs the force-directed graph using d3.forceSimulation, and outputs a complete self-contained HTML file. No delegation to other modules.

The code handles external package nodes via the `language === "external"` check in getNodeColor (returns blue "#79c0ff"), getNodeRadius (returns smaller 6px), and clusterForce (excludes externals from clustering with `if (d.language === "external") return;`). The prepareGraphData function maps `n.language` from the module node to the visualization.

The code implements directory grouping: getGroup() extracts directory paths, clusterForce() nudges same-group nodes together, updateHulls() draws convex hulls around groups. External nodes are excluded from clustering (`if (d.language === "external") return;`). Features include: dir-layout-mode toggle for grid/treemap layout, dir-label-mode for directory labels, and hide-externals toggle.

The computeDirLayout function uses d3.treemap() to create nested rectangles representing directory hierarchy. It builds a trie from directory groups, converts to d3.hierarchy, and lays out with squarified treemap. Area is proportional to node count. External packages are placed in a dedicated strip at the bottom (lines ~112-128 in computeDirLayout).

The code implements click-to-inspect: clicking a node shows only connected edges, outgoing edges colored green (#7ee787), incoming orange (#ffa657), unrelated nodes dimmed to 0.15 opacity. In treemap mode (dirLayoutActive), edges are hidden by default. Nodes are arranged in uniform grid within cells (computeDirLayout). Drag is clamped to cell bounds in tick function. Double-click resets position to original grid location.

The code implements edge tooltips: linkHit creates 12px-wide transparent hit-area lines, and on hover shows tooltip with "Source → Target" label, edge kind, certainty (显示 "inferred (LLM)" for inferred edges), and reason text. The syncHitVisibility function keeps hit-area visibility in sync with visible edges. Legend includes "affects" and "belongs_to" checkboxes, "inferred (LLM)" dashed line entry, and "Hover edge for details" hint.

[UNRELATED] This is a pure type-definition file with no implementation logic. ADR-003 requires implementing impact analysis as graph traversal, but this file only defines TypeScript interfaces (GraphNode, GraphEdge, SemanticDAG). Type definitions don't implement features—they merely describe data structures. No code in this file performs graph traversal or impact analysis.

The ModuleNode interface includes `language: "typescript" | "python" | "external" | "unknown"` which directly supports the ADR-005 decision to track external packages as nodes with an "external" language value. The type definition enables but does not implement the feature.

The GraphEdge interface includes an optional `confidence?: number` field which ADR-011 specifies should be removed from new inferred edges (though kept for backwards compatibility). The type definition correctly allows confidence to be optional, supporting the ADR's backwards-compatibility requirement.

The file defines `Relevance = "related" | "possibly_related" | "unrelated"` and includes it in `EdgeMetadata.relevance`, directly implementing ADR-021's three-tier classification system. The type enables storing relevance data on edges as specified in the ADR.

The module implements ADR-009's module filtering heuristic: it excludes external packages (line 27), uses path-keyword overlap scoring (lines 34-39), boosts modules from frontmatter affects paths (lines 45-50), limits to MAX_MODULES=80 (line 53), and includes existing edges in prompts for edge preservation (lines 77-81). The prompt construction follows the ~4000 token budget target. The module doesn't directly use the Anthropic SDK (that lives in a separate API caller module), but as an "affects" binding, the module's behavior is correctly constrained by ADR-009's filtering and preservation decisions.

The module aligns with ADR-011: the InferredEdge type (lines 16-21) does NOT include a confidence field - only kind, from, to, and reason. The buildAnalysisPrompt function includes the instruction "Only include relationships you are confident about. When in doubt, omit." (line 97) and requires the reason to "cite specific evidence" (line 91). The parseAnalysisResponse function (lines 110-137) validates InferredEdge objects without any confidence field. This matches ADR-011's decision to remove self-assessed confidence and strengthen evidence-based reasoning.

The module aligns with ADR-016: the moduleToLine function (lines 56-61) includes imports (up to 10) in each module line. The buildAnalysisPrompt function includes the strengthened inference rules: tightened "implements" definition requiring direct code changes (lines 85-86), import-verification rule (lines 88-89), barrel/index.ts exclusion (line 87), test file exclusion (line 90), specific evidence requirement in reason field (line 92), and "when in doubt, omit" guidance (line 95). This matches ADR-016's two key decisions.

The module is a barrel file exporting `createSemanticClient` (semantic layer entry point), `analyzeSemantics` (batch inference), and `checkDrift` (edge preservation/drift checking). These align with ADR-009's requirements for Direct Anthropic API integration, batch inference during scan, and the semantic layer functionality. The module's exports form the public API contract constrained by the ADR.

The code implements direct Anthropic API integration with provider auto-detection and graceful degradation (returns null when no credentials). However, it uses LIGARE_ANTHROPIC_KEY and LIGARE_MODEL environment variables instead of ADR_GRAPH_ANTHROPIC_KEY and ADR_GRAPH_MODEL as specified in ADR-009. The model defaults to claude-sonnet-4-20250514 correctly.

The code implements multi-provider switching (Anthropic, Bedrock, Vertex, Compatible) with auto-detection in the correct priority order. The SemanticClient interface includes both analyze() and analyzeWithTools() methods. Progress reporting is handled by the caller (not in this module). Uses LIGARE_PROVIDER and LIGARE_MODEL instead of ADR_GRAPH_PROVIDER and ADR_GRAPH_MODEL.

AnalyzeResult interface correctly includes text, inputTokens, outputTokens, and durationMs fields. The client extracts usage from response.usage and calculates durationMs. However, throughput (tok/s) calculation happens at the caller level, not in this module - which is appropriate. Cache tokens (cacheReadTokens, cacheCreationTokens) are also tracked, extending beyond ADR-012's requirements.

The code correctly fixes the thinking-block bug using `response.content.find((b: any) => b.type === "text")` instead of `response.content[0]`. Thinking blocks are extracted separately via `response.content.find((b: any) => b.type === "thinking")`. The edge tooltip/visualization is handled in the web UI layer, not in this client module.

Truncation detection is correctly implemented: AnalyzeResult includes truncated field, set to true when response.stop_reason === "max_tokens". MAX_TOKENS is 4096 as specified. Dotenv loading and verbose file output are handled at the CLI entry point, not in this client module - which is appropriate separation of concerns.

Thinking block extraction is correctly implemented - AnalyzeResult includes optional thinking?: string field, extracted via response.content.find((b: any) => b.type === "thinking"). The thinking is included in AnalyzeResult so callers (CLI) can write it to verbose log. Running average tok/s calculation happens at the caller level, not in this client module.

The code implements analyzeWithTools() method with ToolDefinition, ToolCall, and ToolUseResult interfaces. The submit_verdict tool is defined with correct schema (adr_id, status, relevance, reason fields). The multi-turn loop correctly handles tool_use blocks, sends tool_result responses, and continues until stop_reason !== "tool_use". Uses LIGARE_* environment variables instead of ADR_GRAPH_*.

checker.ts uses SemanticClient (imported from ./client) to make LLM inference calls via client.analyzeWithTools(). The SemanticClient wraps the direct Anthropic API per ADR-009's requirement to use @anthropic-ai/sdk with API key from environment variable.

checker.ts implements the core drift detection mechanism from ADR-017. The checkDrift function collects bindings from the DAG, sends ADR+code to LLM, and returns aligned/drifting/broken statuses. The interface supports filterAdr, filterModule, and checkAll options matching ADR-017's CLI interface.

ADR-018 binding is "implements" requiring direct implementation. checker.ts delegates to code-summarizer.ts (imports readCodeBlock) rather than directly implementing the three-stage pipeline. However, ADR-024 explicitly evolved this to "On-demand code reading via read_code tool (Phase 2)" - replacing pre-loaded summarization. checker.ts implements ADR-024's pattern (overview + read_code tool + toolHandler). Per ADR evolution rule: when code aligns with a later ADR that explicitly changes an aspect, evaluate earlier ADR as "aligned" (intentional evolution). ADR-024 explicitly changes this approach, so ADR-018 is aligned.

checker.ts implements ADR-019's check-time filtering by using the checkAll option to filter bindings. Lines 47-50 filter out edges with metadata.relevance === "possibly_related" unless checkAll is true, and skippedPreviouslyResolved tracks how many were skipped. This matches ADR-019's design of filtering at check time.

checker.ts implements ADR-021's three-tier relevance by tracking unrelated (prunedEdgeIds), possiblyRelated (possiblyRelatedEdgeIds), and related bindings in result counts. Lines 122-137 process drift.relevance values ("unrelated", "possibly_related", "related") and update edges accordingly. The interface uses this classification to determine what to check (line 47-50 filters out possibly_related unless checkAll is true).

checker.ts implements ADR-022's code-centric batch checking. The groupByModule() function groups bindings by module (lines 130-144), and the main loop processes one LLM call per group with all ADRs that govern that module. This matches ADR-022's restructuring from ADR-centric to module-centric grouping.

checker.ts implements ADR-023's tool-use pattern. It imports SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL from check-prompt.ts, passes it to client.analyzeWithTools() as a tool, parses the tool call results via parseToolCallResults(), and extracts status/relevance/reason from each verdict. This is the core mechanism from ADR-023.

checker.ts implements ADR-024's on-demand code reading. It imports READ_CODE_TOOL, adds it to tools when codeComplete is false (line 68-72), and defines a toolHandler (lines 76-82) that resolves read_code calls by calling readCodeBlock(filePath, blockName). The multi-turn flow matches ADR-024: initial overview + LLM requests specific blocks via read_code + toolHandler returns full code.

The module implements batch inference during scan as required: it iterates over active ADRs (filtering out deprecated/superseded), calls the LLM for each, and merges inferred edges with `certainty: "inferred"`. It uses the `filterRelevantModules` heuristic for token budget management, and preserves existing edges on re-scan by checking `dag.edges[eid]` before adding. The client abstraction is appropriate since the binding type is "affects" (constrained by the decision) rather than "implements".

The module implements progress reporting as required by ADR-010. It defines `AnalysisProgress` interface with `current`, `total`, `adrId`, `status`, `edgesAdded`, and `tokensPerSec`. The `onProgress` callback is invoked before each ADR analysis (status: "analyzing"), after success (status: "done"), and on failure (status: "error"). The client abstraction via `SemanticClient` is appropriate for the "affects" binding type.

The module correctly handles the removal of confidence scoring. Looking at the edge creation code, it sets `certainty: "inferred"` but does NOT set any `confidence` field on the edge object. The `reason` field is captured from the LLM response (`edge.reason`) and stored in `metadata: { reason: edge.reason }`. No confidence score is used for filtering or sorting.

The module implements token metrics as required. `SemanticAnalysisResult` includes `totalInputTokens`, `totalOutputTokens`, and `totalDurationMs`. The `AnalysisProgress` interface includes `tokensPerSec` optional field. The code calculates running average tok/s: `Math.round((result.totalOutputTokens / result.totalDurationMs) * 1000)`. The client returns these metrics via the `response` object from `client.analyze(prompt)`. This is aligned with ADR-012.

The module implements ADR-014 requirements: it accepts `verboseStream` option in `AnalyzeOptions` for controlling where verbose output goes. It checks `response.truncated` and logs "TRUNCATED" label in verbose output, and records a warning in `result.errors[]`: `${adr.node.id}: response truncated (hit max_tokens)`. The dotenv loading happens at CLI entry point (outside this module), which is the correct architectural choice.

The module implements ADR-015 correctly: it accesses `response.thinking` and writes it to verbose log with section header `[${adr.node.id}] thinking`. For tok/s, it calculates a running average across all completed calls (`result.totalOutputTokens / result.totalDurationMs`) and emits it in BOTH the "analyzing" callback (using `runningTps` from prior calls) and the "done" callback (using the updated average). This ensures tok/s is always visible during the run, not just briefly.

The module exports DriftResult interface with status values ('aligned'|'drifting'|'broken') and functions (buildCheckUserMessage, parseToolCallResults) that implement the drift detection mechanism from ADR-017. The system sends ADR text + module source code to LLM for evaluation.

The buildCheckUserMessage function imports and uses buildCodeOverview from './code-summarizer' to create the code section. It includescollapsed block signatures (e.g., 'computeDirLayout — 100 lines') with notes instructing the LLM not to treat collapsed blocks as evidence of absence.

ADR-019's keyword-based computeRelevance was replaced by ADR-021's three-tier LLM classification. The module uses the three-tier relevance ('related'|'possibly_related'|'unrelated') which supersedes ADR-019's binary filtering. CHECK_SYSTEM_PROMPT confirms this evolution.

DriftResult interface includes relevance field with three tiers. VALID_RELEVANCE set enforces 'related'|'possibly_related'|'unrelated'. parseToolCallResults validates against this set. SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL requires relevance in its input_schema - directly implements three-tier classification.

buildCheckUserMessage takes a ModuleNode and AdrBinding[] array, grouping all ADRs for one module into a single user message. It calculates perAdrBudget and truncates ADR bodies to fit within TOTAL_ADR_BUDGET (20000), implementing code-centric batching.

SUBMIT_VERDICT_TOOL has exact schema from ADR-023: adr_id, status (enum), relevance (enum), reason. CHECK_SYSTEM_PROMPT is cleanly separated from user data. parseToolCallResults extracts verdicts from tool_use blocks - implements Phase 1 structured output via tool-use.

buildCheckUserMessage uses buildCodeOverview to create skeleton with block signatures (no bodies), includes note about collapsed blocks and read_code tool. READ_CODE_TOOL has exact schema from ADR-024 with block_name parameter. CHECK_SYSTEM_PROMPT instructs LLM to use read_code before judging collapsed blocks.

The code-summarizer.ts module directly implements ADR-018's three-stage pipeline: (1) extractKeywords function extracts title words, backtick terms, and quoted identifiers; (2) splitIntoBlocks splits code at indent-0 boundaries with imports detection; (3) scoreBlocks implements IDF-weighted scoring and summarizeForCheck allocates budget to include imports first, then highest-scoring blocks, collapsing others to signatures.

ADR-019 explicitly reuses extractKeywords from this module for check-time relevance filtering. The ADR states: "compute a quick keyword relevance score (reusing extractKeywords from ADR-018's code-summarizer)". While the filtering logic itself lives in checker.ts, this module provides the essential extractKeywords function that ADR-019 specifies as the mechanism for computing keyword relevance scores at check time.

The module implements ADR-024's Phase 2 requirements: (1) buildCodeOverview returns imports in full + block signatures (one-liners with line counts) for large files, with small file optimization (under 6000 chars returns full code); (2) readCodeBlock accepts a block name and returns the full block body; (3) reuses existing splitIntoBlocks as specified. The CodeOverview interface has text and isComplete fields matching ADR-024's design.

The AdrFrontmatter interface includes `implements?: string[]` field (line 27), and the parseFrontmatter function parses array values for the `implements` key. This directly implements the ADR-020 decision to have a separate `implements` field that creates `implements` edges.

The AdrFrontmatter interface includes `implements?: string[]` field (line 27), and the parseFrontmatter function parses array values for the `implements` key. This directly implements the ADR-020 decision to have a separate `implements` field that creates `implements` edges.
